sentences.text: F-A-N-I-A-- Fania Ingber-- I-N-G-B-E-R, and Paszt-- P-A-S-Z-T.
In Poland-- Poland, Used to be.
This was the capital of Wolyn.
I don't-- I can't tell you exactly.
I know this-- 20,000 Jews were killed during the war.
And how many exactly, I can't tell you.
I think it was a Christian.
It was a nice Jewish population.
No, all over, all over they city.
Jews, you know, is stores and they work, tailors and shoemakers, all kinds, like always.
They find their things to do.
It's everything [INAUDIBLE] some of them poor, some of them rich, some are them medium class.
Yeah, it was a nice community-- a very nice community.
Oh, they were really very, you know, observant Orthodox, all kind.
Did you have any reformed Jews?
I don't know, because my father was an Orthodox.
That's why I know and I remember, but what about the others I can't tell you.
Friday night, Saturday, Daddy used to go to shul, take the son, the older one.
The younger one, I don't remember.
But it was a Shabbat-- prepared everything like it has to be.
The Shabbat table, it looked beautiful.
It was candles on the table. it was everything how it's supposed to be for Shabbat.
Oh, yeah, always, every Friday in Shabbat nobody use to do anything and relaxed.
But I don't have a picture, sorry.
Oh she was a nice lady-- tall, nice, slim.
I know that father had blue eyes because he-- my children has it.
They-- they look like my father.
And she used to all around, you know, to take them and to make, you know, like, with a bow or sometimes with pins in the back.
This I do remember-- no sheitel.
She was Orthodox, but not too, but she was.
I forgot already, a long time, but we did.
No, no, no, we were quiet, all of us.
I don't remember any hobbies, but I was busy always.
If not the boys, then the girls, and they used to go to school, come back and go to organization.
And my brother belonged there also-- the bigger, older one.
And all the friends used to come to the house, and I went with them.
Yeah, yeah I studied in school.
Then I went to-- when got older, went to public into high school.
But a few-- a few grades.
Yeah, it was religious classes, yes.
Were the same-- in the same time used to have, let's say, an hour a week.
I don't remember how it worked.
No, most I think they were Jewish, because it was like a district, you know, with most of them.
I didn't-- no, I think most of them were Jewish from the school.
In Luck it was always, like everywhere.
Some of them were bad, you know.
It [INAUDIBLE] anti-Semitism, but I don't-- I didn't have anything to do with this.
We had some neighbors, and everybody was OK.
So I can't tell exactly how it was, and I don't remember it.
Oh yeah, there is-- it was altogether, you know.
We used to live one house.
It's not like here, you live in a condo or an apartment.
It was everybody had their own home-- most.
And it was two more-- one two-- two more tenants, but they were Jewish.
Like a one-family house with a basement downstairs, not a living basement, but a basement to keep for the winter and for the summer and dairy and all kind of things, because we didn't have a fridge.
[INAUDIBLE] I think two or three bedrooms and a dining room.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Yeah two-- two bedrooms and a dining room, a big one, a kitchen when you walked in into our-- before you know, like a, hallway, a small one.
From before the war-- what kind of memories?
And the rest I don't care about-- the furniture and all this.
It was nice and beautiful and clean.
But I don't-- the house-- I didn't even going into the house after the war.
He had a shul, which it was the rabbi-- the name of the rabbi who used to come there or [NON-ENGLISH] rabbi.
But I know the shul was named [HEBREW].
I used to go away for the holidays in shul with Mama, but I don't know anything about it.
The women used to sit upstairs.
You have to walk in, and stairs, and the men downstairs.
It was a nice shul-- a very, very nice shul.
I don't remember how fancy it was.
I didn't know what can be, you know, the difference.
But it was a nice shul-- a nice and a big shul.
Yeah, I went with the mother, sure.
My mother didn't go every Saturday, but she used to go from time to time.
But for the holidays, she used to go.
There was not a thing not kosher.
At that time, by us it was the Germans.
The Russians were maybe a year, year and a half, or two years, something like this.
With the Russians everybody used to go to work, and they tried to close, you know, some stores, and the people used to work for-- you know, for the Russian, like-- like in Russia.
Maybe they did succeed by some.
When the Russians were in charge, did you feel any anti-Semitism?
I didn't feel like-- I don't know.
I don't want to say anything because I don't know.
And I don't remember the [? true ?] things.
When the Russians left, and the Germans came in--
It was a war, so they left.
And then the Germans came, and they took right away my father.
They take him away because-- yeah, everybody was home.
But he was standing in the window.
And some Ukrainian came over-- Ukrainian police-- came over and through the window they made to him like this.
So he went over to the bed, and he took the [INAUDIBLE] with the Star of David and the jacket, and they took him away.
It was 3:30 in the afternoon in 1941.
I don't know probably to the Gestapo, or they killed him.
It's not only him they took.
They took a few people there that died.
But he was from the first one.
And we never saw him again.
Yes, the Ukrainians were working for the Germans.
We didn't feel anything, but when they came in, so then we don't-- if we didn't see them, then we went to the ghetto.
Sure, the Ukrainian worked together with the Germans.
Who did it, I don't know.
But they are both-- it was the Ukrainian helped the Germans, and they worked together.
The most important, they took away, and that's it.
Also who was able worked for them, sure.
No, they-- no older people, no, they didn't, no.
Some who had a chance worked with the-- who paid.
Oh, yeah, for sure they pay.
For nothing, they didn't work, and maybe.
To one place, yes, on one side of the city.
They used to live, yeah, usually, yeah.
There was the ghetto, and everybody went.
Oh, the [NON-ENGLISH] ghetto was quite big.
You know-- one part of the city.
It was a special place where-- how you called it?
There was something like a bridge, a small one.
So nobody went, but everybody was afraid.
Yes, and then it was there-- in the ghetto was the hospital.
It was a hospital there, [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] which it was the hospital was there before the war also.
Yeah, not a-- not a big one, because we had-- we had hospitals, a few of them.
But this one was a small one.
It was a few-- a few Jewish doctors that was in the town, and it was nurses Jewish and Polish, oh yeah.
It was another hospital, a big one.
But this was a small one.
It was special, I think, for the-- for the ghetto people-- a small one.
But when they killed, they-- I think they got some from the small villages also to into Luck, and then-- then they killed already.
When it was the [INAUDIBLE], you know, to kill the Jews.
So what was life like in the ghetto?
What did you do in the ghetto?
Just yak, yak, and they said like this, and they said like that.
The Russian going to come back and the opposite, you know.
But there was no work to be done?

For some people, they worked, who, you know-- if they need some people to go to work, they used to give the Judenrat that they have-- they need let's say for today 10 people, 20 people to this place, to the other place, you know.
They used to get some, separately, some passes to get out from the ghetto and [INAUDIBLE] the identification.
I knew them, but I don't remember the names.
But they're Jewish people in the Jewish police force.
They-- or they went, you know, by themself.
They wanted to be in the police, because they thought when they're going to kill, so the police they won't touch.
Who wants to light, who was able to light a candle?
See, some people, they had the candles and some not.
They used to take, like, a piece of string to make like a candle.
But there was no law, because [INAUDIBLE] nobody can afford this.
The people who remained in their houses, so sometimes you had there a few candles, spare one, and sometimes not.
You buy a big box, so you have it for a whole year.
In Poland it was not like this.
If you need two candles, you went out and buy two candles.
She used to light and cried.
Who knows where is my husband?
No, My older brother, he was married.
And the younger was with us.
Then they took him to away, and we also-- we didn't know where he went.
Some people said that they saw him, and he had frozen fingers and feet.
And he was wearing, like, officer boots, you know, stiff one, nice ones, so.
And he couldn't jump or something.
No, no, no, but at the shul, he [INAUDIBLE].
Like a Gentile, because I had two long pigtails.
The face, I think the same.
You know, but younger, and that's it.
Maybe that's why I am alive.
I didn't choose to escape or to-- if somebody would ask me, they'd say, oh, you're going to survive.
It was a big house-- not in our house.
It was a big house, and there's was a couple of families.
Wherever it was a place to sleep.
When you were alone and very hungry, it was hard to share.
When you have to look at the piece of bread to leave it, to eat it now, or to have it later.
You want to take a break?
